briefing.opening: Welcome. We will now begin the climate briefing for June 22, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Warning Signs of Crisis: Threats to Biodiversity and Food Security
sections.content: Over the past 25 years, half of all temperate species have experienced 'local extinction,' a greater impact than that on tropical species, once again underscoring the severity of climate change [2, 65]. Domestically, serious concerns are being reported regarding the spread of apple anthracnose due to climate change, as well as severe damage to pine trees caused by high temperatures, drought, and pests [3, 21]. This situation is shaking global food markets and compelling Korean companies and investors to develop survival strategies [13]. Furthermore, wildfires are becoming 'carbon bombs,' emitting massive amounts of carbon and accelerating the climate crisis, while also threatening river microbial diversity [38, 85]. Research has also emerged indicating that intensifying climate change threatens women's reproductive health [73], necessitating an urgent response to the complex adverse impacts on global ecosystems and human health.

sections.title: ⚠️ Extreme Weather Events and Casualties Striking Globally
sections.content: Half of the world's children are exposed to 'triple climate disasters' such as drought and floods [14], and climate change is directly impacting citizens' lives by causing more powerful hurricanes and prolonging allergy seasons due to dry climate conditions [66, 67]. In Europe, a tragic incident occurred where two children died in southeastern France amidst a fierce heatwave that swept across Western Europe [92], and the UK Met Office issued a red weather warning for an unprecedented heatwave reaching 40°C in June [89, 91]. In Brisbane, Australia, residents were isolated due to floods and heatwaves [93], and extreme weather events are leading to casualties in various parts of the world, with some even suggesting that climate change poses an existential threat to cities like Las Vegas [69]. These climate changes are also affecting consumer prices [68], and there is growing criticism that the denial by politicians and presidential candidates of the difficulties in adapting to climate change is an utterly unacceptable silence [82, 74].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Crisis Response and Accelerated Carbon Neutrality Efforts
sections.content: Domestically, various efforts are underway to address the threats of climate change. The Korea Agro-Fisheries & Food Trade Corporation (aT) and the National Institute of Agricultural Sciences have signed a cooperative agreement to share agricultural information and technology, including collaboration on CA long-term storage demonstrations, in response to climate change and instability in agricultural product supply and demand [1, 5, 9, 10, 11, 12, 16, 17, 20]. Additionally, an international joint research conference was held in Seoul to enhance nuclear power plant safety amidst climate change threats [7, 8], and the Korea Forest Service is striving to strengthen biodiversity conservation capabilities [22], including by hosting expert discussions to find solutions for pine tree management in the climate crisis [15, 19]. Efforts to achieve carbon neutrality are also active. The Gyeongbuk Provincial Council is pursuing an amendment to an ordinance to establish grounds for protecting climate-vulnerable groups [23, 35], and Kongju National University is accelerating the training of carbon neutrality professionals by operating a Level 2 Carbon Reduction Assessment Manager course [27, 28, 33, 37]. Gimcheon City, Yuseong-gu, and Asan Baebang-eup are working to spread a culture of carbon neutrality practice and are achieving greenhouse gas reduction results [18, 30, 34, 39], and Gyeonggi Province is contributing to the establishment of a carbon neutrality practice culture, with participants in its Climate Action Opportunity Income program exceeding 2 million [31]. Furthermore, Shinbo (Korea Credit Guarantee Fund) and major banks are providing 190 billion won in green finance to fully support carbon-neutral companies [26], and Hankook & Company Group is activating a group-wide 'one team' to achieve its 2050 carbon neutrality goal [32], thereby strengthening public-private cooperation.

sections.title: 🌱 Preparing for the Future Through Renewable Energy Transition and Technological Innovation
sections.content: Investments and technological developments for the transition to renewable energy are active both domestically and internationally. GS E&C, in partnership with global investors, is pursuing a 3 trillion won domestic renewable energy development project, aiming to secure 1.5 GW of electricity by 2035 [44, 46, 47, 49, 50, 54, 55, 56, 60, 63, 64]. In the National Assembly, an 'amendment to the Meteorological Act' has been proposed to define and provide meteorological information specialized for renewable energy generation, which is expected to improve the accuracy of power generation forecasts and support stable renewable energy operations [43, 45, 52, 53, 58]. Internationally, 'SunZia,' the largest wind power project in the United States, has been completed [59], and Finger has completed its renewable energy project in Laos [61], expanding renewable energy infrastructure. Meanwhile, innovative technological developments to reverse climate change are also underway, such as research to find the best solvent for dissolving carbon dioxide (CO2) using clean electricity [70, 79], and the Chairman of the Korea Energy Agency emphasized public participation as the key to the success of renewable energy [57]. However, challenges in the transition process also persist; in Europe, electricity prices are still determined by gas prices even in the era of renewable energy [48], and Primoris's Chief Operating Officer (COO) resigned due to renewable energy issues [51].

sections.title: 🌍 The Importance of Global Climate Action and Research Cooperation
sections.content: The international community is strengthening research and cooperation to gain a deeper understanding of climate change and develop effective response measures. Carbon Brief introduced 'Project Cosmos,' the world's largest climate science research database containing 1.8 million climate research academic papers [94, 95, 96, 97, 98, 99], contributing to the dissemination of climate science knowledge. Scientists are also working on adaptation strategies, such as identifying specific coral reef areas resilient to climate change and proposing new conservation methods [76], and tropical fruit farmers in Türkiye are responding to climate change by adopting smart automation technologies [80]. In London, Climate Action Week 2026 has commenced, drawing attention to key events [83], and community-level climate action is also active, with the city of Laval, Canada, investing millions of dollars in flood and climate change response [87]. The message that we can all fight against climate change is being conveyed [78], and interesting research findings, such as beavers contributing more effectively to combating climate change than expected [84], are being published, emphasizing the importance of climate action from various perspectives.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights the severe threats posed by climate change, alongside the simultaneous, multifaceted efforts and innovative initiatives underway both domestically and internationally to overcome them. This is a critical juncture where our sustained attention and action to overcome the climate crisis become even more crucial. This concludes the climate briefing for June 22, 2026.

articles.title: Climate Change Beyond Seasons: Temperate Species Lacked Resilience; Half 'Locally Extinct' Over 25 Years
articles.original_title: 계절 넘어선 기후변화, 온대 생물종 내성은 없었다…25년간 절반 ‘국지적 멸종’
articles.description: A study indicates that temperate species have experienced more extinctions than tropical species over the past 25 years due to climate change. While tropical regions warmed by 1.8 degrees Celsius, temperate regions saw a 3.3-degree increase. Contrary to previous scientific assumptions, temperate species, exposed to seasonal changes, demonstrated less resilience to climate change. Furthermore, 70% of these species were unable to relocate to cooler areas.

summary.phenomenon: Over the past 25 years, half of temperate species have experienced local extinction, a higher rate than tropical species. During this period, tropical regions saw a 1.8-degree Celsius temperature increase, while temperate regions experienced a 3.3-degree increase.
summary.cause: Temperature increases due to climate change have caused the extinction of temperate species. Contrary to previous scientific expectations, temperate species, exposed to seasonal changes, have shown insufficient resilience to climate change.
summary.outlook: It has been observed that 70% of temperate species are unable to relocate to cooler areas, suggesting the potential for further extinctions in the future.
